week_1.what: Python dicts and JSON — how to store and retrieve structured data
week_1.why: The War Room stores country intel as JSON files. This week you build the foundation everything else sits on.
week_1.build: Country Intel File — a dict of 5 countries saved to disk, reloaded, and printed as a formatted brief
week_1.hard_part: Nested dicts trip most beginners. Draw the structure on paper before you code it.

week_2.what: REST APIs and the requests library — how to talk to the internet from Python
week_2.why: The Journalist Agent fetches live news via APIs. This week you learn how that works.
week_2.build: World News Fetcher — call NewsAPI, fetch 5 headlines by country, save to JSON
week_2.hard_part: API keys and .env files feel fiddly at first. The pattern clicks by the end.

week_3.what: SQLite and SQL — a real database instead of JSON files
week_3.why: The War Room archives thousands of news items. Files don't scale — databases do.
week_3.build: News Archive DB — store headlines in SQLite, query by country, keyword, and date
week_3.hard_part: SQL syntax is picky. Start with SELECT * FROM table and build from there.

week_4.what: Your first LLM API call — sending data to Claude and getting back intelligence
week_4.why: This is the moment it clicks. You'll feed real headlines into Claude and get geopolitical summaries.
week_4.build: News Summarizer — fetch headlines → store in SQLite → send to Claude API → get 3-bullet summary
week_4.hard_part: Understanding tokens and cost. Quick math: 1000 tokens ≈ 750 words ≈ a few cents.

week_5.what: RAG — teaching the AI to answer questions using YOUR data, not just its training
week_5.why: The Analyst Agent cross-references news against a vector memory. This week you build that memory.
week_5.build: Chat With Your News Archive — embed headlines into ChromaDB, ask questions, get cited answers
week_5.hard_part: Week 5 is the first real difficulty spike. Embeddings are abstract. Take it one subtopic at a time.

week_6.what: LangGraph — wiring AI agents together as a state machine
week_6.why: Every agent in the War Room is a LangGraph graph. This week you build your first one.
week_6.build: 3-node Research Assistant: Classify → RAG Search → Summarize, with LangSmith tracing
week_6.hard_part: State machines are a new mental model. The concept clicks when you draw the flow chart first.

week_7.what: LangSmith — seeing inside your agent's brain (tracing, cost, debugging)
week_7.why: You can't fix what you can't see. LangSmith makes every agent decision visible.
week_7.build: Fully instrumented agent with @traceable, cost-per-query calculator from trace data
week_7.hard_part: Getting the LANGCHAIN_API_KEY set up. Takes 5 minutes but feels confusing the first time.

week_8.what: MCP — a standard protocol for giving AI agents access to tools
week_8.why: The Journalist Agent fetches news through an MCP server. This week you build that server.
week_8.build: News Feed MCP Server — expose get_headlines() and get_article() as MCP tools
week_8.hard_part: The Host→Client→Server mental model. Draw it on paper. It's not as complex as it sounds.

week_9.what: Multi-agent systems — two AI models working together on a shared task
week_9.why: The War Room uses Gemini to gather news and Claude to analyse it. This week you wire them together.
week_9.build: Journalist (Gemini) + Analyst (Claude) pipeline — topic → brief → confidence-scored assessment
week_9.hard_part: Routing between two different model APIs. Keep the interfaces simple.

week_10.what: Autonomous agents — the system runs without you, alerts you when something matters
week_10.why: The Watcher monitors the world 24/7. The Overseer decides if you need to know.
week_10.build: Watcher + Overseer agents — timer-based monitoring, threshold alerts, human approval gates
week_10.hard_part: Autonomous loops that don't spin out of control. Add a clear termination condition to every loop.

week_11.what: A real dashboard — your War Room as a visual interface you can actually use
week_11.why: All 4 agents running behind one button. This is where the project becomes a product.
week_11.build: Streamlit dashboard — alert feed, Situation Report button, cost tracker, eval suite
week_11.hard_part: Real-time feeds in Streamlit need st.empty() + a loop. A bit fiddly but powerful.

week_12.what: Ship it — turn your project into a portfolio piece the world can see
week_12.why: The War Room is impressive. A well-documented GitHub repo + a 3-min demo video makes it undeniable.
week_12.build: Architecture diagram, professional README, demo video, LinkedIn update, case study post
week_12.hard_part: Writing about your own work is harder than writing the code. Start with bullet points.
